# Boost Your Confidence: 5 Powerful Tips to Overcome Negative Thoughts

Are you struggling with how to overcome negative thoughts or feeling stuck in a cycle of overthinking? Many people battle these inner challenges every day, wondering how to overcome negative thoughts and depression, or simply how to overcome negative thoughts and overthinking. The good news is that overcoming negative thoughts is possible with the right mindset and proven strategies.

At Texas Counseling Center, we believe you can learn how to overcome negative thoughts and unlock your potential by cultivating positive thinking. In this article, we’ll share five actionable tips that empower you to overcome negative thoughts, build resilience, and strengthen your confidence.

## Why Positive Thinking and Overcoming Negative Thoughts Matter

Is positive thinking just about being extra cheerful? Far from it. A positive mindset is vital for mental health, especially for those looking to overcome negative thoughts. Research shows that learning how to overcome negative thoughts and depression improves your physical health, too. Here’s how:

Learning to overcome negative thoughts doesn’t mean pretending challenges don’t exist—it means facing them with strength and optimism, choosing how to respond instead of letting negativity take over.

## 1. Recognize and Challenge Negative Self-Talk

A key step in how to overcome negative thoughts is to first spot when they’re happening. Negative self-talk sounds like an inner critic, always telling you that you’re not enough or that you’ll never get things right.

**How to Overcome Negative Thoughts:**

Each time you interrupt a negative thought, you teach your mind to focus on more supportive, positive ideas.

## 2. Practice Daily Gratitude to Overcome Negative Thoughts

Gratitude is a powerful tool for overcoming negative thoughts. By focusing on what you’re thankful for, you naturally shift your attention from problems to possibilities—an important step when learning how to overcome negative thoughts and depression.

**Simple Ways to Integrate Gratitude:**

## 3. Use Positive Affirmations to Overcome Negative Self-Beliefs

Affirmations are a simple yet effective way to overcome negative thoughts and doubts. When you repeat positive statements regularly, you reinforce constructive beliefs and counter negative thinking.

**How to Use Affirmations:**

## 4. Use Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) to Break Free from Overthinking

If you want to know how to overcome negative thoughts and overthinking, ACT can be a life-changing method. Acceptance and Commitment Therapy teaches you to accept your thoughts without being controlled by them, helping in overcoming negative thoughts and breaking free from endless worry.

**Key ACT Practices for Overcoming Negative Thoughts:**

You can practice these skills daily or with the help of a mental health professional if you’re struggling with how to overcome negative thoughts and depression.

## 5. Build a Positive Community to Support Overcoming Negative Thoughts

Surrounding yourself with compassionate, positive people is crucial when working to overcome negative thoughts. A supportive community can help lift you up and provide encouragement when negativity sets in.

**How to Create a Supportive Environment:**

The Texas Counseling Center is dedicated to helping you build supportive networks, making your journey to overcoming negative thoughts much more achievable.

## Start Your Journey to Overcome Negative Thoughts

Learning how to overcome negative thoughts is a continual process—one that gets easier with each practice. Whether you’re wanting to overcome negative thoughts and depression or are seeking strategies for how to overcome negative thoughts and overthinking, remember you don’t have to do it alone.

At Texas Counseling Center, we guide individuals in overcoming negative thoughts with therapy, tools, and personalized support. If you’re ready to transform your mindset and boost your confidence, our team is here to help.

**Take the First Step Today**

Are you ready to overcome negative thoughts and embrace positive thinking? Contact Texas Counseling Center at [346-440-1800](tel:+13464401800) or visit us online to schedule an appointment. Let’s work together to help you overcome negative thoughts, build your resilience, and create a brighter, more confident future.

You have the power to change your story—starting now.

# How to Overcome Negative Thought Patterns

Negative thought patterns are ways of thinking that focus on the negative. They often lead to stress, anxiety, and self-doubt. These patterns can block your path to happiness and growth. They reinforce themselves through negative self-talk. Fortunately, there are ways to break free from this cycle. In this guide, we’ll explore what negative thought patterns are, why they happen, and steps you can take to overcome them.

![A man in a wheelchair leads a group discussion with three seated participants, holding a tablet and engaging in a collaborative conversation.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201200%20800'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![A man in a wheelchair leads a group discussion with three seated participants, holding a tablet and engaging in a collaborative conversation.](https://12southrecovery.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/Understanding-the-Four-Basic-Steps-of-Intervention-img.jpg)

## What Are Negative Thought Patterns?

Negative thought patterns are habitual ways of thinking that focus on the negative aspects of life. They often lead to stress, anxiety, and depression. These patterns can show up in different ways. Some people expect the worst in every situation. Others make broad assumptions based on a single event. Some might take responsibility for things outside their control, while others see everything in extremes with no middle ground.

These patterns can develop over time due to past experiences, upbringing, or social influences. Negative self-talk often goes hand-in-hand with these patterns, embedding them deeper in your mind. For example, you might think, “I’m not good enough” or “I always fail.” This only reinforces negative beliefs.

Recognizing these patterns is the first step toward overcoming them. But awareness alone isn’t enough. You need practical strategies to change the way you think.

## Recognizing Negative Thought Patterns

Before you can change your thoughts, you need to become aware of them. Many of us go through our days on autopilot, not even realizing how much negativity affects our mood and behavior. Here’s how to start recognizing your negative thought patterns:

By becoming more aware of your thoughts, you can start challenging and changing them. This awareness is the foundation for breaking free from negative self-talk and other harmful thought patterns.

## Challenging Negative Self-Talk

Once you’ve identified your negative thoughts, the next step is to challenge them. Negative self-talk can be particularly damaging because it’s often automatic and deeply ingrained. Here’s how to combat it:

By challenging and reframing your negative thoughts, you can start to shift your mindset from one of self-doubt to one of self-confidence.

## Practicing Positive Thinking

Overcoming negative thought patterns isn’t just about eliminating negativity; it’s also about fostering positivity. Positive thinking can improve your mental health, boost your self-esteem, and help you cope with stress more effectively. Here are some ways to practice positive thinking:

By consciously practicing positive thinking, you’ll start to notice a shift in your overall outlook on life. Positivity can become your new default setting, replacing the old patterns of negativity.

## Building Resilience Against Negative Thoughts

Negative thoughts will inevitably creep in from time to time, but resilience can help you bounce back quickly. Resilience is the ability to recover from setbacks, adapt to change, and keep going despite adversity. To strengthen your resilience, focus on developing a support system. Surround yourself with friends, family, or a therapist who can offer encouragement and perspective when you’re struggling—practice self-care by taking care of your physical, emotional, and mental health. Exercise regularly, eat well, and find ways to relax. Set realistic goals and break them down into manageable steps. Celebrate your progress, no matter how small. Finally, learn from challenges. Instead of seeing obstacles as failures, view them as opportunities to grow. Each challenge you overcome makes you stronger. Building resilience takes time, but it’s a crucial part of overcoming negative thought patterns. The more resilient you become, the less power negative thoughts will have over you.

## Feeling Stuck? How to Rewire Your Mind for Resilience and Hope

Have you ever felt like you’re stuck in the same place, unable to move forward no matter how hard you try? Whether it’s in your personal life, career, or emotional well-being, feeling stuck can be frustrating and overwhelming. But the good news is that your mind has the ability to change, adapt, and grow. With the right strategies and support—like the expert guidance from **Therapist Texas** professionals at Psychological Healing—you can rewire your brain for resilience and hope. Let’s explore how you can break free from limiting thoughts and create a more positive, fulfilling life.

## **Understanding Why You Feel Stuck**

Feeling stuck in life can be frustrating and discouraging, but it’s a common experience that many people face. Whether it’s in your career, relationships, or personal growth, hitting a mental roadblock can make it seem like progress is impossible. These emotional barriers don’t appear out of nowhere—they often develop from deep-seated thought patterns, past experiences, and internal fears.

![5 Powerful Thought Exercises to Stop Feeling Trapped in Life](https://psychologicalhealing.net/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Exercises-1024x683.webp)

### **Common Reasons You May Feel Stuck**

#### **Negative Thought Patterns That Repeat Over Time**

Our thoughts have a powerful impact on our emotions and actions. When you constantly tell yourself that you’re not good enough, that things will never change, or that success is out of reach, you create a mental barrier that prevents growth. These self-defeating thoughts often develop from past failures, negative experiences, or even influences from family and society. The longer these thought patterns persist, the harder it becomes to break free from them.

#### **Unresolved Past Experiences That Shape Present Emotions**

Trauma, disappointment, and past failures can weigh heavily on the mind. Sometimes, negative experiences from childhood or past relationships continue to influence the way we think and behave today. For example, if you’ve been repeatedly told that you’re not capable of achieving something, you might internalize that belief and allow it to hold you back. Unresolved emotions such as guilt, regret, or fear of repeating past mistakes can create a cycle of hesitation and self-doubt.

#### **Fear of Failure or Uncertainty About the Future**

Fear is one of the biggest obstacles to progress. The fear of making the wrong decision, failing at a goal, or stepping outside of your comfort zone can keep you feeling stuck. Uncertainty about the future can also create anxiety, making it difficult to take action. When you focus too much on what could go wrong, you may end up avoiding challenges altogether, which only reinforces the feeling of stagnation.

#### **A Lack of Self-Confidence or Motivation**

Confidence and motivation are key factors in moving forward. When you don’t believe in your ability to succeed, it’s easy to stay in a cycle of inaction. Low self-esteem can stem from past criticisms, failures, or even unrealistic societal expectations. Motivation, on the other hand, can be difficult to maintain if you don’t have a clear sense of purpose or direction. Without motivation, even small tasks can feel overwhelming, making it difficult to gain momentum toward your goals.

### **Breaking Free from Mental Obstacles**

Acknowledging these challenges is the first step toward overcoming them. Instead of blaming yourself for feeling stuck, recognize that these barriers are often learned behaviors and patterns that can be changed. By identifying the specific thoughts and fears holding you back, you can begin to reframe your mindset and create a path toward progress.

## **The Science of Rewiring Your Brain**

The idea that you’re “stuck” is often just a perception—one that can be changed with the right approach. Your brain has an incredible ability to adapt and rewire itself, thanks to a process called neuroplasticity. This means that with intentional effort, you can reshape your thought patterns and develop a healthier mindset.

### **How to Rewire Your Brain for Growth**

**Mindfulness and Self-Awareness**Practicing mindfulness helps you become more aware of your thoughts and emotions. Instead of reacting automatically to negative feelings, mindfulness allows you to pause, observe your thoughts, and choose a more constructive response. Techniques such as meditation, deep breathing, and journaling can help increase self-awareness and reduce stress.

**Challenging Automatic Negative Thoughts**Many of our negative thoughts are automatic—they appear without us even realizing it. When you catch yourself thinking, “I’ll never succeed” or “Things will never change,” challenge those beliefs. Ask yourself:

Replacing negative self-talk with more balanced, encouraging thoughts can gradually reshape the way you see yourself and your abilities.

**Creating New Habits**Habits play a huge role in shaping our mindset. The more you engage in positive behaviors—such as setting goals, practicing gratitude, or exercising—the more your brain reinforces those behaviors. Over time, these habits become second nature, making it easier to maintain an optimistic outlook.

## **Building Resilience Through Mental Shifts**

Resilience is the ability to recover from setbacks, adapt to change, and keep moving forward despite challenges. It’s not something you’re born with—it’s a skill that can be developed.

# How to Overcome Negative Thinking Patterns in Mental Health Recovery

### Understanding and Addressing Negative Thought Patterns

Negative thinking patterns can significantly hinder mental health recovery, perpetuating cycles of anxiety, depression, and self-doubt. By understanding these patterns and implementing effective strategies, individuals can foster a healthier, brighter outlook on life.

## Recognize and Label Negative Thoughts

![Understanding Negative Thoughts: The Psychological Basis](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66f1f500f433503f990af072/67a45d6feeae6e3e49f6c8a0_3a1cdbfbc6b4fb990f16555d48f7474969401a12.png)

![Understanding Negative Thoughts: The Psychological Basis](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66f1f500f433503f990af072/67a45d6feeae6e3e49f6c8a0_3a1cdbfbc6b4fb990f16555d48f7474969401a12.png)

### Why do negative thoughts come to mind, and what psychological processes are involved?

Negative thoughts often emerge from our brain's survival mechanism, which includes a critical inner voice that detects potential threats and recalls past mistakes. This process is rooted in cognitive distortionsâirrational thought patterns that exaggerate negatives or downplay positives. When these cognitive distortions become habitual, they can create a vicious cycle leading to increased levels of anxiety and depression.

### Understanding Cognitive Distortions

Common cognitive distortions, such as all-or-nothing thinking, emotional reasoning, and catastrophizing, distort our perception of reality. For example, individuals may feel that if they can't achieve perfection, they should abandon their efforts entirely. These unhealthy thought patterns contribute not only to mental distress but can also impede personal relationships and reduce overall life satisfaction.

### The Importance of Awareness and Labeling

Awareness of negative thought patterns is crucial. By labeling thoughts as they arise, individuals can create mental distance, allowing for clearer thinking. Techniques such as keeping a thought journal or engaging in mindfulness practices help in identifying recurring distortions.

Incorporating therapies like cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) can further aid in restructuring negative thoughts into more balanced perspectives. This process fosters improved emotional resilience and can significantly enhance mental well-being.

## Mindfulness Techniques to Interrupt Negative Thought Patterns

![Mindfulness: Techniques to Detach from Negativity](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66f1f500f433503f990af072/67a45d6feeae6e3e49f6c892_8ed371b5d53b2c4710cdf38ee408946073e8e786.png)

![Mindfulness: Techniques to Detach from Negativity](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66f1f500f433503f990af072/67a45d6feeae6e3e49f6c892_8ed371b5d53b2c4710cdf38ee408946073e8e786.png)

### How can I detach from negative thoughts using mindfulness practices?

Detaching from negative thoughts is an essential skill for improved mental well-being. Mindfulness practices provide a structured approach to recognize and manage these thoughts. Start by cultivating self-awareness through meditation, which allows you to observe your thoughts without judgment.

**Identify Cognitive Distortions**: Recognize common negative thinking patterns like catastrophizing or all-or-nothing thinking. This awareness is crucial in addressing faulty logic that fuels negativity.

**Cognitive Restructuring Techniques**: Challenge your negative thoughts by questioning their validity. Ask yourself: What evidence do I have for this thought? This can help replace these negative beliefs with more realistic perspectives.

**Reflective Journaling**: Keeping a diary of your thoughts can uncover recurring themes and help identify emotional triggers. Writing down your experiences can provide clarity and distance from negative feelings.

**Surround Yourself with Positivity**: Engage with uplifting content and supportive individuals. Surrounding yourself with positive influences fosters a more optimistic environment.

### Additional Mindfulness Techniques

Practicing mindfulness includes activities such as:

These techniques are not merely about avoiding negative thoughts but learning to manage them constructively, promoting overall mental health.

## Utilizing Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) for Recovery

![Cognitive Behavioral Therapy: Restructuring Your Mind](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66f1f500f433503f990af072/67a45d6feeae6e3e49f6c898_f6e8acaa77f5266561df4cbe9471937609e09011.png)

![Cognitive Behavioral Therapy: Restructuring Your Mind](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66f1f500f433503f990af072/67a45d6feeae6e3e49f6c898_f6e8acaa77f5266561df4cbe9471937609e09011.png)

### Applications of CBT

Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is an established approach for addressing negative thought patterns, particularly useful for individuals in recovery. CBT provides practical techniques to assist in recognizing and altering distorted beliefs that may impede progress. Therapists guide individuals to spot cognitive distortions, such as overgeneralization or catastrophizing, which contribute to anxiety, depression, and self-doubt.

### Challenging Negative Thought Patterns

One effective method within CBT involves questioning the validity of negative thoughts. This process includes scrutinizing automatic responses and challenging irrational beliefs by substituting them with more balanced perspectives. For instance, instead of thinking, "I canât do anything right," one might reframe it as, "I have made mistakes, but I also have successes and can learn from this."

### Cognitive Restructuring

Cognitive restructuring is a central technique in CBT that helps individuals replace harmful thought processes with healthier ones. By utilizing tools such as thought journals, individuals can track their thoughts and identify patterns of negativity. For example, labeling cognitive distortions helps create distance from them. Engaging in mindfulness exercises further encourages individuals to acknowledge their thoughts without judgment, fostering a flexible mindset that aids in recovery.

In summary, the strategies offered by CBT, such as identifying, challenging, and restructuring negative thoughts, empower individuals to rebuild their cognitive frameworks, supporting sustained recovery and mental well-being.

## Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) as an Alternative Approach

![Acceptance and Commitment Therapy: Embrace Your Thoughts](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66f1f500f433503f990af072/67a45d6feeae6e3e49f6c89b_cafb194eedc17b5ac0c3fb3753753f7ab1c602ef.png)

![Acceptance and Commitment Therapy: Embrace Your Thoughts](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66f1f500f433503f990af072/67a45d6feeae6e3e49f6c89b_cafb194eedc17b5ac0c3fb3753753f7ab1c602ef.png)

### Role of ACT in mental health recovery

Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) is a unique approach that emphasizes accepting negative thoughts and feelings rather than fighting against them. This acceptance allows individuals to move forward in their mental health recovery. By fostering a commitment to personal values and goals, ACT encourages individuals to engage in behaviors aligned with their aspirations rather than being trapped by cognitive distortions.

### Mindful awareness

A key component of ACT is cultivating mindful awareness. This practice helps individuals observe their thoughts and feelings without judgment, enabling a clearer perspective on their experiences. Mindfulness techniques, such as deep breathing and guided meditations, defuse the emotional intensity of negative thoughts, allowing individuals to respond more flexibly to life's challenges.

### Changing relationship with thoughts

ACT encourages a shift in how individuals relate to their thoughts, recognizing them as mere thoughts rather than absolute truths. This shift can disarm the influence of negative thinking, promoting a healthier mindset. By practicing acceptance, individuals can learn to engage with their thoughts more constructively, paving the way for a resilient and balanced mental health journey.

## The 5 C's of Negative Thinking and Their Effect

![The 5 C's of Negative Thinking: Understanding Their Impact](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66f1f500f433503f990af072/67a45d6feeae6e3e49f6c895_b155cfa01e4f4b7accf2ed67427c2185f88c2150.png)

![The 5 C's of Negative Thinking: Understanding Their Impact](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66f1f500f433503f990af072/67a45d6feeae6e3e49f6c895_b155cfa01e4f4b7accf2ed67427c2185f88c2150.png)

### What are the 5 C's of negative thinking, and how do they impact mental patterns?

The 5 C's of negative thinkingâ**COMPLAINING, CRITICIZING, CONCERN, COMMISERATING, and CATASTROPHIZING**âform a cycle of negativity that profoundly affects mental patterns. Each of these habits fosters a mindset rife with self-doubt and hopelessness, often skewing oneâs perception of reality.

This cycle is aggravated by cognitive biases like **Confirmation Bias**, where individuals seek evidence to support their negative views, and **Negativity Bias**, which emphasizes adverse experiences over positive ones. Consequently, leaders entrenched in these thought processes may become overconfident or overly reliant on initial negative impressions, significantly impairing decision-making.

The culmination of these behaviors transforms workplace culture, as critical attitudes can deteriorate morale and hamper teamwork. Recognizing and addressing these 5 C's is essential for nurturing a healthier and more constructive mindset, both personally and professionally.

## Practical Methods for Combating Negative Thoughts

### What exercises can help stop negative thinking?

To combat negative thinking, several effective exercises can be practiced. Keeping a thought record is particularly beneficial; in this exercise, individuals note down their triggers and associated feelings, providing clarity and understanding of their anxiety patterns.

Practicing self-compassion is also vital. Techniques like the worry tree guide you through addressing your anxieties step-by-step, helping to reframe and mitigate negative thoughts.

Mindfulness exercises, especially focusing on your five senses, can significantly bring your attention back to the present moment, thus reducing tendencies to ruminate excessively. Engaging in physical activities is another practical approach. Simple exercises such as walking, yoga, or even stretching can enhance physical well-being and also distract from obsessive thoughts.

Additionally, maintaining healthy lifestyle habits like balanced nutrition and adequate sleep contributes to overall mental health, creating a stronger foundation for managing negative thought patterns. Incorporating these exercises into your daily routine can foster a more positive mindset over time.

## Building Resilience and Positive Self-Talk in Recovery

### Cultivating Resilience

Building resilience is vital in overcoming negative thinking patterns, especially during recovery. Start by recognizing that setbacks are a natural part of life. Embrace mistakes as learning opportunities instead of failures. One can foster a resilient mindset by practicing gratitude, which involves acknowledging positive experiences and acts of kindness daily. This helps counterbalance negative thoughts and reinforces a positive self-view.

### Using Affirmations

Affirmations can be a powerful tool in reshaping your internal dialogue. Phrases like "I am capable" or "I am getting better every day" serve to combat negative self-talk. Repeating positive affirmations regularly can help foster a constructive mindset, reinforcing self-worth and encouraging a focus on progress. This practice can gradually change the narrative you have about yourself and build a more optimistic outlook.

### Role of Support Systems

Engaging with supportive individuals is essential in managing negative thinking. Talking about your feelings with trusted friends or family can provide relief and valuable perspectives. These relationships help challenge distorted thoughts, reminding you that youâre not alone in your struggles. Surrounding yourself with positive influences can create a nurturing environment, crucial for maintaining resilience during tough times.

#### How to get rid of negative thinking patterns?

To get rid of negative thinking patterns, it's essential to recognize and differentiate your thoughts from your identity, understanding that "you are not your thoughts." Start by identifying your negative thought patterns and performing reality checks on those thoughts to assess their accuracy. Instead of suppressing these thoughts, aim to create a healthy distance from them and challenge any distorted beliefs that may have originated from external influences. Incorporate positive affirmations to reprogram your thinking and focus on what you can control in your life. Additionally, consider seeking professional support through therapy if negative thinking becomes overwhelming or interferes significantly with your daily life.

## Impact of Negative Thinking on Addiction Recovery

### Role of Negative Thoughts in Relapse

Negative thinking can significantly hinder recovery from addiction. Many individuals battling addiction often experience automatic negative thoughts, such as feelings of helplessness and self-doubt. These thoughts can precipitate a relapse, manifesting during three stages: emotional, mental, and physical. Emotional relapse might be marked by poor self-care, while mental relapse involves a strong obsession with substance use. Understanding these thought patterns is crucial for preventing relapse and maintaining sobriety.

### Strategies for Maintaining Sobriety

To combat negative thoughts, individuals can adopt various proactive strategies. These include practicing mindfulness techniques to stay present and engaged, utilizing positive affirmations to counter any self-sabotaging beliefs, and seeking support from friends or support groups. Maintaining a routine filled with uplifting people and activities can also help redirect focus away from negativity toward recovery goals.

### Addressing Cognitive Distortions

Identifying and reframing cognitive distortionsâsuch as overgeneralization or catastrophizingâis vital in managing negative thinking. Professionals often recommend Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) as an effective method to challenge harmful beliefs and encourage balanced thinking. Techniques like writing thought journals can also help individuals recognize and restructure negative thought patterns, fostering emotional resilience essential for sustained recovery.

| Strategy | Description | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Mindfulness | Focusing on the present moment | Reduces anxiety |
| Positive Affirmations | Reinforcing constructive self-talk | Boosts self-esteem |
| Thought Journaling | Writing down thoughts to identify cognitive distortions | Aids in recognizing thought patterns |

## Fostering a Positive Mindset for Lifelong Well-being

Overcoming negative thinking patterns is a crucial component of mental health recovery. By implementing practices such as mindfulness, CBT, and ACT, alongside cultivating resilience and positive self-talk, individuals can rise above negativity and foster enduring mental well-being. Remember, the journey towards a positive mindset is ongoing, and seeking support from professionals, friends, and loved ones is always encouraged. Embrace the present moment, challenge those negative thoughts, and awaken to a healthier, happier life.

The Four Keys to Overcoming Negative Thinking…for Good
======================================================

Do you struggle with negative thinking? Maybe you have a harsh inner critic that crushes your confidence? Perhaps you get caught in worry, stress, anxiety, depression? Or have a pessimistic view of life?

If that sounds like you, then you probably know how painful the effects can be. Negative (unhelpful) thinking patterns can have a strong and sometimes devastating impact on us. Clouding our mood, straining our relationships and draining our vitality. Creating a burden on our health, our work… really all aspects of our lives. Overcoming negative thinking – it’s not as easy as just pushing negative thoughts away and replacing them with positive ones. (If it were that simple, we would all be on cloud nine, wouldn’t we?) So, while we often know it’s happening to us, we don’t always know what to do about it.

If you’re new to my world, you may not know this – before I became a [mental strength and mindfulness teacher](https://melliobrien.com/about/) I was in some pretty dark places with my thinking. I battled bulimia and depression throughout my teens. My mind became my own worst enemy and I ended up hating myself and even questioning whether it was worth continuing to live. What helped me turn things around, little by little, was cultivating the skills to turn mental struggles into mental strength. And so, it’s something I care about sharing with all my heart.

Learn Mental Strength
---------------------

In this article, I’ll help you to recognise what the common negative thinking patterns are, explain why it’s happening to you, and cover the evidence-based keys to rising above it. I can tell you from my own experience that no matter where you are starting from, it is possible to turn things around, cultivate mental strength, and live a more happy, meaningful and fulfilling life. It’s not about learning how to stop negative thoughts; it’s about changing the way we relate to our thoughts. As well as training our minds to work in more empowering and helpful ways.

With _The Four Keys_, some persistence and practice, I believe anyone can break free of being stuck in mental struggles for good. I now spend my life teaching other people the skills and tools that turned my life around. These skills are all evidence-based and have worked for me and thousands of others. I trust these tools will work for you too.

What is negative thinking and why is it so painful?
---------------------------------------------------

Negative thought patterns are repetitive, unhelpful thoughts. They directly cause what we could describe as ‘negative’ (unwanted or unpleasant) emotions. And can contribute to anxiety, depression, stress, fear, unworthiness, loss of confidence and more.

Some negative thoughts are conscious. That is, we are aware of them.

Other thoughts are negative unconscious thoughts. These are thoughts, words or images that often run in the background of our awareness, in our subconscious. Even though we may not be fully aware of these thought patterns, they still can have a profound impact on us. Often these thoughts stem from our internal belief systems and our old conditioning.

Negative thinking is often conversational. It’s a conversation we have with ourselves mentally, but it can also impact the way we speak out loud to others. It can also be situational. It can skew the way we anticipate situations, the way we view and experience them in the present moment and the way we interpret them afterwards.

The Weight of the Glass
-----------------------

A professor teaching stress management principles walks around an auditorium. In her hand, is a glass of water. She asks her students, “How heavy is the glass of water I’m holding?” The students shout out their guesses. She smiles and replies. “From my perspective, the absolute weight of this glass doesn’t matter. What does, is how long I hold it for. If I hold it for a minute or two, it feels quite light. If I hold it for an hour or so, it’s going to make my arm really start to ache. If I hold it for a whole day, my arm will be in agonizing pain, and would probably start shaking before going completely numb and I’ll drop the glass. In each case, the glass doesn’t change. The heaviness depends on how long I hold it.”

She continues “Your stresses and worries are like this glass of water. Think about them for a while and nothing much happens. Think about them all day long, and you will feel completely overwhelmed by negativity and anxiety and you may even become paralysed. Incapable of doing anything else until you drop them.”

So, in this way, we can begin to understand that it’s not the negative thoughts themselves that are the biggest part of our problem. It’s how long we are carrying them.

The four big types of negative thinking patterns
------------------------------------------------

### Anxiety and Worry

“My life has been full of terrible misfortunes, most of which never happened”. ~ Michel de Montaigne

Worry is when the mind projects into an imagined future and conjures up scenes and thoughts about what could go wrong. Here it often creates ‘what if’ scenarios and often slips into catastrophizing – that is, imagining the worst-case scenarios.

Sometimes worry and anxiety takes the form of imagining or expecting that bad things will happen. To us, our loved ones, career or finances. So we can end up feeling a huge amount of anxiety even if nothing bad is actually happening at that moment.

If that’s you, you may also find yourself thinking a lot of “I should, I need to, I have to” type of thoughts. Or running through the to-do list over and over again until you feel overwhelmed and stressed.

Anxiety and stress disorders can result when we hold onto stressful and worrying thoughts too tightly and for too long. Some of us can experience panic attacks. A feeling of intense anxiousness that produces feelings of dread and doom. Anxiety can cause physical symptoms, like heart palpitations, hyperventilation, insomnia and trembling. The [latest data on anxiety disorders](https://adaa.org/understanding-anxiety/facts-statistics) reveals they impact 40 million adults in the United States alone.

### Ruminating on mistakes

Ruminating on mistakes, problems and misfortunes from the past often creates a heavy load of negativity. We may play ‘bad’ choices, ‘wrong’ actions or embarrassing moments over and over in our minds. Like that thing you wish you didn’t say at the party. Or the mistake you made five years ago that you won’t let go of.

When we’re stuck in a thought loop about what we’ve done in the past, we can’t fully embrace the present moment, nor move forward into the future with confidence and strength. This pattern can keep us stuck in cycles of feeling worthless, guilty or ashamed.

There is nothing innately wrong with reflecting on past experiences. This is how we can learn, grow and mature as people. Negativity only arises when we dwell on a situation repeatedly without any true intention to learn, solve things or move forward. Rumination ends up feeling more like just punishing ourselves. We wish things were different and we beat ourselves up about it. Like holding the weight of the glass for too long, rumination can end up causing us agonising inner pain and hold us back from being our best selves and living our best lives.

### Self-criticism and never feeling good enough

We all have a voice in our heads. It talks to us all day long in the form of constant thoughts. Sometimes this voice is helpful. Alerting us to things that we can do to achieve our goals or solve problems. But often, this inner voice is our own worst enemy. Sometimes it’s a downright bully! Constantly putting us down, telling us we are not enough. That self-critical voice raises doubts, points out our flaws and questions the way we are living our lives.

Most of us would never speak to someone else the way we speak to ourselves. Our inner critic can be incredibly harsh and mean. When we make a mistake or have a setback, it often goes into overdrive. Bringing even more suffering to an already difficult time.

All this negative self-talk and self-criticism can crush our confidence, shatter our self-esteem and make us feel unworthy and unhappy. Sometimes, we can feel so debilitated by these feelings we become depressed or even suicidal.

Sometimes self-criticism can be disguised as self-improvement. While there is nothing wrong with having goals and improving our circumstances, it’s a different headspace when the motivation is that we don’t feel like we’re ‘enough’ as we are. In this state, we’re constantly pushing our body and mind to the limits – trying to ‘be someone’ in the eyes of the world. When we’re chasing achievements, trying to attain status, and recognition for these reasons, we’re actually just trying to compensate for feeling ‘not good enough’ feelings. This path leads to burnout and emptiness eventually.

The path to feeling true self-worth is through releasing those thoughts that tell you are deficient and need to prove yourself. By doing this, you can learn to feel whole and live an authentic and meaningful life right where you are.

### Focus on the negative and fixating on problems

Negative thoughts often revolve around stewing on what’s going wrong in our lives. Our attention becomes fixated on, and often exaggerates, the difficult or unpleasant aspects of situations, people and events. Here our minds will often downplay or overlook what’s going well in life and put the majority of focus on what’s not.

For example, you may have a wonderful family, food to eat, a great job and a safe and cosy home. But when your car breaks down, it’s all you can think about and focus on all week long. All week you are frustrated, angry and depressed because of the car. You allow the situation with the car to dominate your thinking and negative emotions arise as a result. Forgetting about all the great things you love about your life.

When we’re so absorbed in what’s wrong, we’re unable to notice what’s right. Rather than allowing our focus to expand into what is going well at the same time, we remain only with what isn’t. Zeroing in and dwelling on unpleasant situations make us feel greater levels of stress, unhappiness, frustration, anxiety and negativity in daily life.

#### Why do we experience negative thought patterns?

This tendency for the human mind to focus on the negative is completely normal (it’s not just you). It is caused by what is known as the brain’s ‘negativity bias.’

Say you do a presentation at work and everyone in your team has to give you feedback. If five people give great compliments on your work, but one person criticises it, which feedback do you think you’ll remember most? Which one might keep you up at night ruminating? Let’s face it, most likely the criticism. That focus on criticism is very normal, and it’s not your fault. It gets highlighted in your mind because of the [negativity bias](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1037/1089-2680.5.4.323).

This bias has been hard-wired into the human brain through evolution. In early times, the world was a dangerous place. It was important for us to learn from negative experiences so that we could outwit predators and avoid threats around us. These were real and thinking about all the possible ‘go wrongs’ kept us safe.

Your brain registers negative experiences very quickly, highlighting and storing them in memory. It doesn’t really care how you’re going to feel about it later. It’s in ‘survival first’ mode.

Today, we don’t live in a world where there are constant dangers like there were thousands of years ago. But our brains still operate the same way. We overinflate and hold on to negative things even when our lives don’t depend on it. The problem with the negativity bias for us these days is that over the long term we can develop a growing tendency to be pessimistic, stressed and negative. The good news is, we don’t have to resign ourselves to this. We can train our minds to adopt more empowering and uplifting thinking patterns.

#### The four keys are not about learning how to stop negative thoughts, but how to _transmute_ them.

[![Image 3: how to stop negative thoughts transmute them girl blowing dandelion](https://melliobrien.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/08/how-to-stop-negative-thoughts-transmute-them-girl-blowing-dandelion-300x225.jpg)](https://melliobrien.com/the-four-keys-to-overcoming-negative-thinkingfor-good/how-to-stop-negative-thoughts-transmute-them-girl-blowing-dandelion/)People are often at a loss with how to stop negative thoughts from occurring. And this is part of the problem. They often try many different ineffective ways to get rid of their negative thoughts. This includes trying to push them away, distract themselves from them, ‘numbing,’ or ‘drowning their sorrows.’ Only to later find themselves still stuck, and even sinking deeper in negativity. Research shows that while struggling or arguing with, trying to drown out or push away negative thoughts may give short term relief; it only amplifies them over the long term. And actually, makes things worse. Hence the saying, what we resist persists.

What is absolutely critical, and something a lot of people miss is that **you can’t just replace negative thoughts with positive thoughts**. If we could do that, everyone would be okay. Overcoming negativity is about changing the way that we relate to our thoughts, as well as being able to steer our focus in more helpful ways.

It’s also important to note that you should not view _The Four Keys_ I’m going to share with you as a ‘quick fix.’ Although you may get some immediate relief by using these tools, overcoming negative thinking for good is going to take a little practice and persistence on your part. But it’s well worth the investment of your time and effort. As you shed negative thinking, you’ll feel better, unlock more of your potential and live a happier, healthier and more meaningful life.

Once you understand what causes negative thinking and learn to apply the four following mental skills, you can start to shift from mental struggle to mental strength and transform the quality of your life from the inside out.

#### Key One: Recognise Negative Thoughts and let them go (Mindfulness)

The first key to overcoming negative thinking is to practice being the _observer_ of our thoughts rather than being ‘hooked’ by them. Once we learn to recognise and ‘unhook’ from negative thoughts as they arise, we become free from any impact they may have had on us.

### How do we get stuck in negative thinking? About cognitive fusion

What do I mean by getting ‘hooked’ by thoughts? A strong attachment (getting hooked to) our thoughts is known as ‘cognitive fusion.’ When we are fused with our thoughts, we tend to get stuck in them. We tend to take thoughts very, very seriously. We believe them. We buy into them. And we will often play them out.

For example, imagine you wake up one day, and when you look out the window, you see it’s raining. At that moment a thought may come into your head that says, “what a dreadful day.” Now is it true that the day is dreadful? No, of course not! It’s simply raining. However, if you believe that thought, then guess what you will probably have? That’s right, you will probably have a dreadful day! If you get fused to a thought like that, it will probably generate negative feelings like grumpiness, bitterness or resentment.

**So, we don’t need to know how to stop negative thoughts. The problem is not that we have negative thoughts. The problem comes when we get hooked by our thoughts and believe our thoughts are true.**

### Getting unstuck. The process of cognitive defusion

The process of unhooking from, or ‘stepping back from’ thoughts is called ‘cognitive defusion.’ When we are no longer stuck in thoughts, they lose their power to pull us into unpleasant emotions, make us reactive or hold us back in any way.

Cognitive defusion allows us to see thoughts as simply that – thoughts. Merely snippets of sound and language. Mental events moving through the mind all the time. Just like the weather passes through the sky. Not ‘the truth’ or ‘the way things are’ Just a bunch of words going through the mind.

Once we see thoughts as mental events, we don’t take them so seriously. We don’t automatically believe or obey them. If we find them valuable or helpful, we might pay attention to them or act on them. If we don’t, we just let them go. We begin to shift our whole relationship with thoughts. Holding them lightly means we are not so thrown around or affected by them.

Let’s go back to the example of the rain. Imagine you’re lying in bed again, and you look out and you see that it’s raining. Once again, the thought arises “what a dreadful day”. Instead of buying into that thought, you simply observe it. You watch the thought “what a dreadful day” arise and fall away. And since you don’t attach to it, take it seriously or believe it, it generates no negativity and passes by easily. You’re left free to lay there relaxed and at peace, enjoying the pitter-patter of the rain on the roof.

As you can see, the ability to recognise unhelpful thinking and step back from it is incredibly liberating! It can change the quality of your whole day and indeed your whole life. It has mine.

### The ‘name it to tame it’ technique for instant cognitive defusion

‘Name it to tame it’ is a very powerful, simple, tried and tested technique. It was developed by author and psychiatrist [Dr. Daniel Siegel](https://www.keepingintouchbc.com/blog/2016/07/11/dr-dan-siegel-can-name-can-tame) to help us untangle from thoughts without struggling with them.

Here’s how you do it. When an unhelpful thought pattern arises, (or an associated negative emotion) we simply mentally ‘label’ the thought pattern and then we let it go.

For instance, as unhelpful thoughts pass by, you might say to yourself “ahhh negative thoughts arising” or you can even shorten it by just saying “thinking.” In this way, you unhook yourself from believing the thought and take a step back from it. It’s almost like saying to the thought “I see you! I see what you’re up to!”

You can even bring in a little bit of humour and have fun with this. Mentally, noting something like “ahhh radio doom and gloom is playing again” or “dark clouds of thought alert!”

So, after we mentally note, we let it go. By ‘let it go’ I mean to stop giving it your full attention. Instead, shift your mind back to what is happening in the present moment and give that your focus. Tune into your sense perceptions, what you can feel, see, or smell.

As soon as we name the mental pattern, and shift the focus back to the present moment, we’ve initiated cognitive defusion! If you like to geek out on [neuroscience](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10751965), the act of naming thoughts kicks in the smartest part of the brain, the frontal cortex. The frontal cortex is reflective and helps you zoom out with greater perspective.

### The way you name it matters

One important thing to note with ‘name it to tame it’. Make sure that when you mentally note you’re using a soothing, kind and even playful tone of voice. This helps you to soothe your nervous system and invite compassion and resilience into that moment instead of aggression or struggle. We’re not going to battle with the mind here. We are gently training it into new neural pathways of peace and ease. At a biological level, when you ‘name it to tame it’ in a soothing voice you even get a squirt of soothing neurotransmitters in the brain! This brings feelings of calm, ease and comfort.

### Mindfulness

_“Let us not look back in anger or forward in fear, but around in awareness.” ~ James Thurber_

Just beneath the waves of our thoughts, emotions and conditioning, there is a deep ocean of awareness in each one of us. A source of calm, wisdom, compassion and incredible inner strength. It is always available to us as a place of refuge. Mindfulness is the practice of cultivating that awareness.

Through mindfulness, we build our capacity to stay grounded in that greater awareness, instead of being so caught up in, and tossed around by, the surface waves of thoughts and emotion.

Regular mindfulness meditation is the best way to build the muscle of mindfulness. There’s a free meditation audio you can try for yourself below. Meditation has been shown to decrease stress, depression and anxiety. People who practice meditation report higher overall levels of satisfaction with life than others. In fact, Harvard researcher and psychologist Matt Killingsworth found what makes people most happy is being fully present in the moment. He says the more our minds wander, the more unhappy we become. There is so much power in this simple practice. On top of regular meditation practice, you can also use the following simple practice to bring it with you into your daily life.

### The three-breath pause: a mindfulness practice for daily life.

One simple yet very powerful way to bring mindfulness into your daily life is through using the three breath pause technique.

The three-breath pause can help you find your calm centre and inner strength in any moment in life.

A good analogy for the power of this practice can be found in bullfighting. It is believed that in the midst of the bullfight, the bull can find his own particular area of safety in the arena…his querencia. Once there, he can reclaim his wits and find his strength and power. He may only be there for a few seconds – but those seconds make all the difference.

As long as the matador can keep the bull busy, reactive and stressed continuously, that matador stays in control. But when the bull finds querencia, when he finds his centre, he gathers his strength and focus. From the matador’s perspective, at this point, the bull is truly dangerous, as he has taken his power back. The matador is no longer in control.

This is a great metaphor for how we can find our centre through the practice of the mindful three breath pause. In the arena of our daily life, we can often find ourselves stuck in our mental struggles, consumed by busyness and stress. Thrown around by reactivity. This can affect every aspect of our lives. Our relationships and health can suffer, our mood plummets, and we don’t perform as well at work.

In the same way, a bull takes back his power by pausing to gather himself and tap into his inner strength, so can we.

### Give it a try

Whenever you feel reactive, overwhelmed or just need some more calm in the midst of all the crazy, you too can find your centre and proceed with greater awareness and clarity of mind. All it takes is to pause and centre yourself with three, deep, slow mindful breaths. Even if you’re right in the middle of the ‘ring’ of stress and reactivity.

As you take the three breaths, let your full attention rest on the feeling of the breath coming in and out of your body. Pay close attention to every sensation, and let any other thoughts fall away for these moments.

#### Key Two: Practice self-compassion

Imagine if we met for the first time. And I started our conversation by saying, _“I don’t know why you’re even trying to overcome negative thinking. You’ll never change. You look like someone who doesn’t follow through with anything.”_

No doubt if I spoke to you that way it wouldn’t feel very nice. It’s pretty demoralising. And not just that, but you most likely would not want to listen to me anymore because it doesn’t feel helpful or healthy to be spoken to that way!

Of course, I would never talk to you that way in reality. But how often do we talk to ourselves like that? This inner critic actually forms the core for much of our inner struggles and stress. Now here’s the thing, this kind of negative self-talk is not a sign of any deficiency on your part or anything strange about you, it’s a sign of having a normal human mind.

### The human mind is a survival machine

For our caveman ancestors, life was very difficult and dangerous. In order to survive, you had to stay in the tribe. If you got kicked out it wouldn’t be long before a predator or starvation got hold of you. And so, fitting in with the group, having the approval of the rest of the tribe was essential to our survival. 

So how does the mind function to keep you in the tribe? It’s constantly questioning – am I fitting in? Am I special enough? Am I good enough? Am I doing anything that could get me rejected? It also compares us to everyone else in the tribe constantly to see how we are measuring up. Driven by an impulse to make sure we get approval, the inner critic comes in and demands us to be better ‘like the prettier, smarter, more successful or stronger ones.“ It pushes us, bullies us, criticises us. It tells us we’re not enough. All in an attempt to keep us safe.

### Ancient brains in a modern world

The problem is, now we don’t live in tribes, and through technology and media we are now comparing ourselves to a huge number of people all over the world. Images that depict impossible standards of airbrushed and hyped up perfection. These days we are being bombarded with these kinds of images through the media and advertising. When we’re constantly scrolling and surfing, we see images of people who seem more attractive, more happy, more fun, more successful than us. It’s easy to feel like we aren’t measuring up, not enough, not worthy. And then we can often tip into anxiety or depression. In fact, the rise in social media has been shown to be one of the [probable reasons](https://www.healthline.com/health-news/social-media-use-increases-depression-and-loneliness#Does-social-media-cause-depression?) for a huge surge in depression around the world. Presently [1 in 5 people](https://pulsetms.com/resources/around-world/) in the United States are diagnosed with depression.

### From self-criticism to self-compassion

The most powerful skill to unwind the inner critic and cultivate inner peace is the skill of self-compassion. Self-compassion involves training our inner critic to be more like a kind and supportive inner coach. Now, usually, when people first hear about self-compassion they have hesitations. Why? Many people believe that if they don’t crack the whip with harsh self-talk, they won’t be motivated to make changes and reach goals. However, research shows just the opposite. Self-limiting and self-critical thoughts sap our motivation and initiative. Whereas self-compassion and kindness increase them. When we learn to be kinder to ourselves we also become more resilient to challenges and stress. We’re more productive and able to overcome bad habits and addiction. And have more fulfilling relationships with others.

### How to ease the voice of the inner critic and develop self-compassion

Start by practising self-talk that sounds warm, friendly and kind. The way we speak to ourselves can often be incredibly harsh. Especially if something has gone wrong or we made a mistake. Let your inner voice take on the tone of a supportive friend or coach.

If you catch yourself sounding unfriendly, see if you can take a pause and change the tone. Say kind and encouraging things to yourself like, “_Good on you for giving that a go honey_,” or, “_That was a very kind/honest/good thing you did mate_,” or, “_Hey, it’s okay, you did your best_.” If you don’t find terms of endearment like ‘honey’ or ‘mate’ useful then of course just drop them and find your own way with this.

When you’re having a hard time, try talking to yourself the way you would speak to a loved one who is hurting. Say soothing words to yourself. Like, “_It’s ok, everyone makes mistakes sometimes. You’re human_,” or, “_Take it easy darling,_” or, “_Oh this is a tricky moment. May I be kind to myself in this tough time_.”

### Do the things that nourish you

Part of self-compassion is being a caretaker of your own needs. Another way to practice self-compassion is through your actions. Try focusing on doing more things that nourish you, feed your soul and genuinely make you happy rather than doing things to ‘make it in the eyes of the world’ or to get ‘likes’, approval or status in the eyes of others.

It could be helpful to make a list of the things that recharge the batteries, relax and rejuvenate you. The things that light you up, bring you joy and feed the soul.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Negative thought patterns create a cycle of self-doubt and anxiety, reinforcing feelings of inadequacy and hindering personal growth, as highlighted in cognitive-behavioral therapy.
A strong support system is crucial for resilience; without it, individuals may feel isolated, intensifying feelings of hopelessness and making recovery more challenging.
Chronic exposure to stressful environments can lead to burnout and emotional fatigue, making it difficult to cultivate resilience and cope with everyday challenges.
Cultural stigma around mental health can prevent individuals from seeking help and openly discussing their struggles, ultimately hindering their ability to build resilience.
Fear of failure can paralyze individuals, preventing them from taking risks and pursuing goals, which is essential for building resilience and personal development.
